misc.title: #ONEPIECE1181 In Chapter 1179, we see Imu transforming in the castle. In the new chapter, Imu isn't fully visible in the Joyboy scene. And the structure in the flashback scene could really fit with Ve
misc.text: #ONEPIECE1181 In Chapter 1179, we see Imu transforming in the castle. In the new chapter, Imu isn't fully visible in the Joyboy scene. And the structure in the flashback scene could really fit with Vearth. Perhaps that's why he shrouds his body in darkness, because he doesn't want his followers to see him like that.

misc.title: イム様の“天罰剣(ネメシス)”🗡️ ミホークのコレじゃん！ ネメシス”はギリシャ神話に登場する「復讐」の女神。人間の高慢に対する神の怒り(天罰)を擬人化した存在。 「夜」の女神ニックスの娘。 ミホークの剣の名前は「夜」。 ここも繋がる。#今週のワンピ .Log【ワンピース考察】 (@manganouA) 以前、グルグルの目の共通点についてポストしたけど、イム様とミホークな雰囲気が似過ぎてるな🤔
misc.text: イム様の“天罰剣(ネメシス)”🗡️ ミホークのコレじゃん！ ネメシス”はギリシャ神話に登場する「復讐」の女神。人間の高慢に対する神の怒り(天罰)を擬人化した存在。 「夜」の女神ニックスの娘。 ミホークの剣の名前は「夜」。 ここも繋がる。#今週のワンピ .Log【ワンピース考察】 (@manganouA) 以前、グルグルの目の共通点についてポストしたけど、イム様とミホークな雰囲気が似過ぎてるな🤔🌀#今週のワンピ イム様、もといネロナの家系は現在まで続いてるのかな？イム様の子孫説ある？ — https://nitter.net/manganouA/status/2047620985864716429#m

misc.title: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone
misc.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone with real talent or intelligence doesn’t go around showing it off for no reason. That idea is perfectly embodied by "Hawk-Eye Mihawk". He’s not the kind of person who flaunts his strength on his own. That’s probably why he called Zoro “a weakling” at first—because Zoro was the one trying to prove himself by challenging him. And when you hear “爪 claws,” another thing that comes to mind is the Hoof of the Flying Dragon. That’s the symbol of the "Celestial Dragons". Hancock, for example, desperately wants no one to see that claw-shaped mark, because it’s the brand of a slave. So if Mihawk is someone who “hides his claws,” then maybe that also suggests he wants to hide the claws he himself carries. A hawk that hides its claws. In other words, it could even be a metaphor for Mihawk hiding the fact that he carries "Celestial Dragon" blood. Stussy Sama - Dawn&Dusk (@DawnStussy) x.com/i/article/204221152086… — https://nitter.net/DawnStussy/status/2042241260086612180#m

items.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 There’s a Japanese proverb that Connie, from the Sorbet Kingdom, also referred to: “能ある鷹は爪を隠す: A skilled hawk hides its claws.” It means that someone with real talent or intelligence doesn’t go around showing it off for no reason. That idea is perfectly embodied by "Hawk-Eye Mihawk". He’s not the kind of person who flaunts his strength on his own. That’s probably why he called Zoro “a weakling” at first—because Zoro was the one trying to prove himself by challenging him. And when you hear “爪 claws,” another thing that comes to mind is the Hoof of the Flying Dragon. That’s the symbol of the "Celestial Dragons". Hancock, for example, desperately wants no one to see that claw-shaped mark, because it’s the brand of a slave. So if Mihawk is someone who “hides his claws,” then maybe that also suggests he wants to hide the claws he himself carries. A hawk that hides its claws. In other words, it could even be a metaphor for Mihawk hiding the fact that he carries "Celestial Dragon" blood.
